front: What are the three enzyme components of the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex and which coenzyme does each use?
back: The complex has three catalytic components working in sequence. E1 (pyruvate dehydrogenase) uses thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP) to decarboxylate pyruvate. E2 (dihydrolipoyl transacetylase) uses lipoic acid (lipoamide) and coenzyme A to transfer the resulting two-carbon acetyl group to CoA. E3 (dihydrolipoyl dehydrogenase) uses FAD and then NAD+ to reoxidize the reduced lipoamide, producing NADH.

front: In the pyruvate dehydrogenase mechanism, what is the specific job of thiamine pyrophosphate on the E1 subunit?
back: TPP attacks the carbonyl carbon of pyruvate and stabilizes the negative charge that develops when the carboxyl group leaves as CO2, forming a resonance-stabilized hydroxyethyl-TPP intermediate. TPP is the universal coenzyme for decarboxylation of alpha-keto acids, so the same chemistry runs in alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase and transketolase. This shared dependence is why thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency impairs all of these enzymes at once.

front: What is the role of the lipoamide swinging arm in the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex?
back: Lipoic acid is covalently linked to a lysine residue on E2, creating a long flexible lipoamide arm. It accepts the two-carbon unit from TPP on E1, becomes acetylated, then physically swings to deliver the acetyl group to coenzyme A and to hand electrons to E3. This substrate channeling keeps intermediates from diffusing away, and the two thiol groups of the reduced arm are the target of arsenite poisoning.

front: How many NADH and CO2 molecules does the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex generate per molecule of glucose?
back: PDH converts each pyruvate to one acetyl-CoA, releasing one CO2 and one NADH per pyruvate. Because glycolysis yields two pyruvate per glucose, PDH produces 2 NADH and 2 CO2 per glucose before the citric acid cycle even begins. Those 2 NADH are worth about 5 ATP total, roughly 2.5 ATP each, through oxidative phosphorylation.

front: Which carbon of pyruvate is released as CO2 by the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex?
back: The carboxyl carbon (C1), the most oxidized carbon of pyruvate, is removed as CO2. The remaining two carbons form the acetyl group that is attached to coenzyme A as acetyl-CoA. This is the first CO2 released inside the mitochondrion during glucose oxidation; the remaining CO2 molecules come from the citric acid cycle.

front: To what enzyme family does the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex belong, and which relatives share its mechanism?
back: PDH is an alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase. Its relatives are alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase of the citric acid cycle and the branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase of amino acid catabolism. All three require the same five coenzymes (TPP, lipoic acid, coenzyme A, FAD, NAD+) and share an identical E3 subunit, so an E3 defect or thiamine deficiency disables all three simultaneously.

front: What signals activate PDH kinase to switch off the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex?
back: PDH kinase phosphorylates and inactivates the complex when the cell is energy-rich. It is stimulated by high ratios of ATP to ADP, NADH to NAD+, and acetyl-CoA to CoA, all reporting that acetyl-CoA and reducing power are abundant. Pyruvate, the substrate, and ADP inhibit the kinase, keeping PDH active when fuel still needs to be oxidized.

front: How do calcium and insulin turn the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex on?
back: PDH phosphatase removes the inhibitory phosphate and reactivates the complex. Calcium strongly stimulates the phosphatase, so in contracting muscle a rise in matrix calcium boosts acetyl-CoA production to meet demand. Insulin also activates the phosphatase in tissues such as adipose, matching the fed state in which glucose-derived pyruvate should be oxidized rather than conserved.

front: Why is blockade of pyruvate dehydrogenase harmful despite the reaction being a single decarboxylation step?
back: Because the reaction is irreversible and is the only path for carbohydrate-derived pyruvate to become acetyl-CoA, blocking it strands pyruvate. The excess pyruvate is shunted to lactate, causing lactic acidosis, and glucose-dependent tissues such as the brain lose their acetyl-CoA supply. This pattern appears in inherited PDH deficiency and in thiamine deficiency, both marked by elevated blood lactate.

front: How does inherited pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency present, and why can a ketogenic diet help?
back: PDH deficiency prevents conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, so pyruvate accumulates as lactate, producing congenital lactic acidosis and neurological impairment because the brain depends on glucose oxidation. A ketogenic diet, high in fat and very low in carbohydrate, helps because fatty acids and ketone bodies are converted to acetyl-CoA independently of PDH. This supplies the citric acid cycle while bypassing the blocked step.

front: Why is the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex called the link or transition reaction, and where does it occur?
back: It is called the link, bridge, or transition reaction because it connects cytosolic glycolysis, which ends at pyruvate, to the citric acid cycle, which consumes acetyl-CoA in the mitochondrial matrix. Pyruvate is first transported across the inner mitochondrial membrane, then PDH decarboxylates it to acetyl-CoA inside the matrix. Sitting at the mitochondrial gateway, it commits carbohydrate carbon to aerobic oxidation.

front: How does acetyl-CoA coordinate the two competing fates of pyruvate in the mitochondrion?
back: Mitochondrial pyruvate can be decarboxylated by pyruvate dehydrogenase to acetyl-CoA or carboxylated by pyruvate carboxylase to oxaloacetate. Acetyl-CoA is the switch: when it builds up, as during fasting from fatty acid oxidation, it inhibits PDH and allosterically activates pyruvate carboxylase. This diverts pyruvate toward oxaloacetate for gluconeogenesis or for replenishing the citric acid cycle instead of making more acetyl-CoA.

front: Which three steps of one citric acid cycle turn generate NADH, and which single step generates FADH2?
back: Three dehydrogenases reduce NAD+ to NADH: isocitrate dehydrogenase, alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, and malate dehydrogenase. One step, succinate dehydrogenase, reduces FAD to FADH2. Each full turn therefore yields 3 NADH and 1 FADH2, along with 1 GTP from succinyl-CoA synthetase and 2 CO2.

front: Counting oxidative and substrate-level phosphorylation, how much ATP does one full turn of the citric acid cycle ultimately yield?
back: Per turn the cycle makes 3 NADH (about 2.5 ATP each, or 7.5), 1 FADH2 (about 1.5 ATP), and 1 GTP (equivalent to 1 ATP), giving roughly 10 ATP using modern P/O values. With the older textbook values of 3 ATP per NADH and 2 per FADH2, the total is 12 ATP per turn. Nearly all of the energy is captured first as reduced electron carriers rather than as direct GTP.

front: How much ATP does the citric acid cycle contribute per molecule of glucose?
back: Each glucose yields two pyruvate, hence two acetyl-CoA and two turns of the cycle. At about 10 ATP per turn, that is roughly 20 ATP per glucose from the cycle alone, or 24 by the older 12-per-turn count. This is separate from the 2 NADH made by pyruvate dehydrogenase and the ATP and NADH from glycolysis.

front: What activates and what inhibits isocitrate dehydrogenase, the rate-limiting enzyme of the citric acid cycle?
back: Isocitrate dehydrogenase is the main flux-control point of the cycle. It is allosterically activated by ADP and calcium, which signal low energy charge and muscle contraction, and inhibited by ATP and NADH, which signal that energy is plentiful. This links cycle speed directly to the mitochondrial NADH to NAD+ ratio and the cellular energy charge.

front: Which effectors inhibit citrate synthase, the entry enzyme of the citric acid cycle?
back: Citrate synthase condenses acetyl-CoA with oxaloacetate and is inhibited by ATP, NADH, and succinyl-CoA, all indicators of a full cycle or high energy, as well as by its product citrate. Its rate is also limited by the supply of its substrates acetyl-CoA and oxaloacetate. Slowing it prevents wasteful use of acetyl-CoA when downstream intermediates are already backed up.

front: How is alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase regulated within the citric acid cycle?
back: Alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase is inhibited by its own products succinyl-CoA and NADH in classic product inhibition, and by high ATP, while calcium activates it. Because it shares the mechanism and coenzymes of the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex, it is likewise impaired by thiamine deficiency and arsenite. It is one of three calcium-activated matrix dehydrogenases that accelerate the cycle during contraction.

front: Which three mitochondrial dehydrogenases does calcium activate at once, and why is this useful?
back: Calcium activates isocitrate dehydrogenase and alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase within the cycle plus the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex that feeds it. Because a rise in matrix calcium is the same signal that triggers muscle contraction, this coordinately increases acetyl-CoA supply and cycle flux exactly when ATP demand rises. It is a feed-forward mechanism matching fuel oxidation to work.

front: Why does succinate dehydrogenase use FAD rather than NAD+ as its electron acceptor?
back: Succinate dehydrogenase oxidizes an alkane-like carbon-carbon single bond in succinate to the double bond of fumarate. This oxidation releases too little energy, its reduction potential is not negative enough, to reduce NAD+, but it is well matched to reduce the tightly bound FAD. The resulting FADH2 passes electrons straight to ubiquinone through Complex II.

front: What is the consequence of a succinate dehydrogenase mutation for both the citric acid cycle and the electron transport chain?
back: Succinate dehydrogenase uniquely serves both the citric acid cycle, oxidizing succinate to fumarate, and the electron transport chain as Complex II, feeding electrons to ubiquinone. A loss-of-function mutation blocks the cycle at succinate and removes an FADH2 entry point into the chain. Accumulated succinate can leak to the cytosol and stabilize hypoxia-inducible factor, a pseudohypoxia effect linked to hereditary paragangliomas and pheochromocytomas.

front: How is the GTP produced in the citric acid cycle converted into usable ATP?
back: Succinyl-CoA synthetase carries out the only substrate-level phosphorylation of the cycle, forming GTP, or ATP directly in some tissues, as succinyl-CoA becomes succinate. The enzyme nucleoside diphosphokinase then transfers the phosphate to ADP: GTP plus ADP gives GDP plus ATP. The GTP is therefore energetically equivalent to one ATP.

front: Trace the carbon count through the citric acid cycle from oxaloacetate back to oxaloacetate.
back: A two-carbon acetyl group condenses with four-carbon oxaloacetate to form six-carbon citrate. Isocitrate dehydrogenase releases one CO2 to give five-carbon alpha-ketoglutarate, and alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase releases a second CO2 to give four-carbon succinyl-CoA. The remaining four-carbon units regenerate oxaloacetate, so the cycle is balanced and oxaloacetate acts catalytically.

front: What does it mean that citric acid cycle intermediates act catalytically, and why does that require anaplerosis?
back: Oxaloacetate and the other intermediates are regenerated every turn, so even a small pool can process large amounts of acetyl-CoA, acting catalytically rather than being consumed. However, intermediates are constantly withdrawn for biosynthesis, so replenishing reactions called anaplerosis must refill the pool or the cycle stalls. The principal anaplerotic reaction is pyruvate carboxylase producing oxaloacetate.

front: Besides pyruvate carboxylase, what anaplerotic reactions replenish citric acid cycle intermediates?
back: Transamination or deamination of glutamate yields alpha-ketoglutarate and of aspartate yields oxaloacetate, refilling the cycle from amino acids. Catabolism of odd-chain fatty acids and of certain amino acids such as valine, isoleucine, and methionine produces propionyl-CoA, which is carboxylated and rearranged into succinyl-CoA. These inputs top up the four- and five-carbon intermediates drained for biosynthesis.

front: What is cataplerosis, and give examples of intermediates withdrawn from the citric acid cycle?
back: Cataplerosis is the removal of cycle intermediates for biosynthesis, the counterpart of replenishing anaplerosis. Citrate is exported to the cytosol for fatty acid and cholesterol synthesis, alpha-ketoglutarate and oxaloacetate are transaminated to glutamate and aspartate for amino acids and nucleotides, and succinyl-CoA supplies heme synthesis. Because these withdrawals deplete oxaloacetate, anaplerosis must keep pace to sustain the cycle.

front: Which classic competitive inhibitor blocks succinate dehydrogenase, and what did its effect demonstrate?
back: Malonate is a structural analog of succinate that competitively inhibits succinate dehydrogenase. Because it resembles the substrate but cannot be dehydrogenated, adding more succinate overcomes the inhibition, which is how competitive inhibition was classically demonstrated. Malonate causes succinate to accumulate and halts the cycle at that step.

front: How does fluoroacetate poison the citric acid cycle through lethal synthesis?
back: Fluoroacetate is converted by the cell into fluoroacetyl-CoA and then, by citrate synthase, into fluorocitrate. Fluorocitrate is a potent inhibitor of aconitase, so citrate accumulates and the cycle is blocked at that step. This phenomenon, in which a harmless compound is metabolized into a toxin, is called lethal synthesis.

front: Why does diverting oxaloacetate into gluconeogenesis slow the citric acid cycle and promote ketone formation?
back: Every turn of the cycle begins by condensing acetyl-CoA with oxaloacetate, so oxaloacetate availability sets how fast acetyl-CoA can enter. During fasting, oxaloacetate is pulled into gluconeogenesis, lowering cycle capacity. Acetyl-CoA from fatty acid oxidation then backs up and is diverted into ketone bodies, which is why the saying goes that fat burns in the flame of carbohydrate.

front: Why is the mitochondrial NADH to NAD+ ratio described as the master brake on the citric acid cycle?
back: Three cycle enzymes are slowed by high NADH: isocitrate dehydrogenase and alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase are directly inhibited, and malate dehydrogenase is held back because its reaction is near equilibrium and NADH is a product. NADH accumulates when the electron transport chain cannot reoxidize it, as when oxygen is scarce or ATP demand is low. A rising NADH to NAD+ ratio therefore automatically slows the cycle, tying its rate to oxidative phosphorylation and oxygen supply.

front: How does citrate link the citric acid cycle to the control of glycolysis and fat synthesis?
back: When the cycle and energy charge are high, citrate accumulates and some is exported to the cytosol. There it allosterically inhibits phosphofructokinase-1, slowing glycolysis so glucose is not needlessly broken down. Cytosolic citrate is also cleaved to acetyl-CoA to begin fatty acid synthesis and activates acetyl-CoA carboxylase, so citrate signals a fed, energy-rich state favoring storage over catabolism.

front: How many electron pairs does one acetyl-CoA transfer to carriers in the citric acid cycle, and in what form?
back: Complete oxidation of one acetyl-CoA transfers four pairs of electrons, that is eight electrons, to carriers: three pairs to NAD+ forming 3 NADH and one pair to FAD forming 1 FADH2. These reduced carriers hold most of the energy of the acetyl group and later drive the electron transport chain. The cycle also makes 1 GTP by substrate-level phosphorylation and releases two carbons as 2 CO2 per turn.

front: How many protons does each electron transport chain complex pump per pair of electrons donated by NADH?
back: For each electron pair from NADH, Complex I pumps about 4 protons, Complex III about 4 protons, and Complex IV about 2 protons, moving roughly 10 protons total from the matrix into the intermembrane space. Complex II pumps none. This proton stoichiometry is the physical basis for calculating ATP yield per NADH.

front: How many protons must pass through ATP synthase to make one ATP, and how does this set the yield per NADH?
back: About 4 protons are needed per ATP: roughly 3 to rotate the c-ring enough to synthesize one ATP plus about 1 to import phosphate and exchange ADP for ATP across the membrane. Since a NADH drives about 10 protons out, 10 divided by about 4 gives roughly 2.5 ATP per NADH, and a FADH2 driving about 6 protons gives roughly 1.5 ATP. This is why the modern values are 2.5 and 1.5 rather than the older 3 and 2.

front: Which cofactors carry electrons inside Complex I, and where do those electrons go next?
back: Complex I, NADH-ubiquinone oxidoreductase, oxidizes NADH by first passing two electrons to a bound flavin mononucleotide (FMN), then through a chain of iron-sulfur (Fe-S) clusters, and finally to ubiquinone, reducing it to ubiquinol. The energy released drives the pumping of about 4 protons. FMN can accept two electrons at once and pass them one at a time to the one-electron Fe-S centers.

front: What redox centers does Complex IV use to reduce oxygen to water?
back: Complex IV, cytochrome c oxidase, contains heme groups cytochrome a and cytochrome a3 plus two copper centers, CuA and CuB. It collects four electrons one at a time from cytochrome c and uses them with four matrix protons to reduce one O2 to two water molecules. It also pumps about 2 additional protons per electron pair, and its a3-CuB binuclear site is where cyanide and carbon monoxide bind.

front: What is the Q cycle carried out by Complex III?
back: Complex III, cytochrome bc1, transfers electrons from two-electron ubiquinol to one-electron cytochrome c. Because the donor carries two electrons and the acceptor takes one, Complex III uses the Q cycle, recycling ubiquinone through two sites in the membrane and effectively pumping about 4 protons per electron pair. Its redox centers include cytochromes b and c1 and a Rieske iron-sulfur protein.

front: Why do the electron transport chain complexes act in the order I, III, IV rather than a random sequence?
back: Electrons flow spontaneously from carriers of more negative reduction potential to those of more positive potential, releasing free energy at each step. The sequence NADH, Complex I, ubiquinone, Complex III, cytochrome c, Complex IV, oxygen follows steadily increasing reduction potential from about -0.32 V for NADH to +0.82 V for oxygen. Arranging carriers along this gradient lets the chain release energy in usable increments to pump protons.

front: Using standard reduction potentials, how much free energy is released when electrons pass from NADH to oxygen?
back: The NAD+/NADH couple has a standard reduction potential of about -0.32 V and the O2/H2O couple about +0.82 V, an overall difference of about 1.14 V. Applying delta-G equals -nF times delta-E with n equal to 2 and F equal to 96485 C/mol gives about -220 kJ/mol, roughly -52 kcal/mol. This large release powers proton pumping and, indirectly, the synthesis of several ATP per NADH.

front: Which side of the inner mitochondrial membrane becomes acidic and positively charged during electron transport?
back: Protons are pumped from the matrix into the intermembrane space, so that space and the cytosolic face of the membrane become more acidic and more positive, while the matrix becomes alkaline and negative. This difference in proton concentration and charge is the proton-motive force. Protons then flow back down the gradient into the matrix through ATP synthase.

front: Which component of the proton-motive force dominates across the inner mitochondrial membrane?
back: The proton-motive force has a chemical component, the pH gradient, and an electrical component, the membrane potential. In mitochondria the electrical membrane potential dominates, typically about 150 to 200 millivolts, because the matrix pH is kept close to neutral while pumped protons carry charge outward. The two components together store the energy that ATP synthase uses.

front: How does the binding-change mechanism of ATP synthase produce ATP?
back: As protons flow through the F0 c-ring, the attached central stalk rotates within the F1 head and forces each of the three catalytic beta subunits through three conformations: loose, which binds ADP and Pi, tight, which forms ATP, and open, which releases ATP. The energy of the gradient is used mainly to release the tightly bound ATP rather than to form the bond itself. One full 360-degree rotation drives synthesis of about 3 ATP.

front: Why can cytosolic NADH from glycolysis not be oxidized directly by the electron transport chain?
back: The inner mitochondrial membrane is impermeable to NADH and NAD+, so cytosolic NADH cannot cross to reach Complex I. Its electrons are instead carried inward by shuttle systems, the malate-aspartate shuttle or the glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle. Which shuttle is used decides whether those electrons enter as matrix NADH, about 2.5 ATP, or as FADH2, about 1.5 ATP.

front: How do the malate-aspartate and glycerol-3-phosphate shuttles differ in reversibility and tissue distribution?
back: The malate-aspartate shuttle, found in heart, liver, and kidney, regenerates matrix NADH and is reversible, so it operates only when the matrix NADH to NAD+ ratio permits, giving about 2.5 ATP per cytosolic NADH. The glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle, prominent in skeletal muscle and brain, feeds electrons to FAD, is essentially irreversible, and works even when the matrix is highly reduced, but yields only about 1.5 ATP. This difference is why glucose can net 30 or 32 ATP depending on the tissue.

front: Why did the estimated ATP yield per glucose fall from 36 to 38 down to about 30 to 32?
back: Older calculations assumed exactly 3 ATP per NADH and 2 per FADH2 from presumed whole-number stoichiometry. Measurements show that proton pumping and the cost of transporting ATP, ADP, and phosphate give non-integer ratios of about 2.5 ATP per NADH and 1.5 per FADH2. Recomputing with these values, and accounting for which shuttle carries cytosolic NADH, lowers the total to about 30 to 32 ATP per glucose.

front: Which electron transport inhibitors act at Complex I, and what is the downstream effect?
back: Rotenone, an insecticide and fish poison, along with amobarbital (amytal) and piericidin, block electron transfer from Complex I to ubiquinone. NADH-linked substrates can no longer be oxidized, so NADH builds up and NADH-driven ATP synthesis stops. Electrons from FADH2 can still enter at Complex II, so some oxygen consumption and ATP production persist.

front: Why does rotenone block oxidation of NADH-linked substrates while succinate can still be oxidized?
back: Rotenone inhibits Complex I, the entry point for NADH electrons, so any fuel feeding electrons through NADH is blocked. Succinate donates its electrons through Complex II directly to ubiquinone, bypassing Complex I entirely. Succinate oxidation therefore continues, at reduced yield, even with rotenone present, a classic way to localize where an inhibitor acts.

front: Which electron transport complex do cyanide, azide, and carbon monoxide inhibit, and how do their binding preferences differ?
back: All three block Complex IV, cytochrome c oxidase, at the heme a3-CuB center, stopping electron transfer to oxygen. Cyanide and azide bind the oxidized ferric (Fe3+) form of heme a3, whereas carbon monoxide binds the reduced ferrous (Fe2+) form. With oxygen no longer able to be reduced, the whole chain backs up and oxidative ATP synthesis halts, forcing cells onto anaerobic glycolysis.

front: Which inhibitor blocks Complex III, and where does it act in the Q cycle?
back: Antimycin A inhibits Complex III by binding the quinone-reduction (Qi) site, blocking electron transfer from cytochrome b to ubiquinone and interrupting the Q cycle. Electrons can no longer reach cytochrome c, so carriers upstream become reduced and those downstream become oxidized. Like other chain inhibitors it collapses proton pumping and stops ATP synthesis.

front: At the molecular level, how do chemical uncouplers such as 2,4-dinitrophenol dissipate the proton gradient?
back: Uncouplers like 2,4-dinitrophenol and FCCP are small lipophilic weak acids that act as protonophores. They pick up a proton on the acidic intermembrane side, diffuse across the membrane, and release it in the matrix, short-circuiting the gradient. The electron transport chain keeps running, even faster, but the energy is released as heat instead of being captured by ATP synthase.

front: Why does an uncoupler increase oxygen consumption while an electron transport inhibitor decreases it?
back: An uncoupler collapses the proton gradient that normally restrains electron transport, so the chain runs fast and oxygen consumption rises, though no ATP is made. An inhibitor physically blocks a complex, stopping electron flow so oxygen consumption falls and ATP synthesis stops. In short, uncouplers speed respiration but waste it as heat, whereas inhibitors shut respiration down.

front: Why was 2,4-dinitrophenol dangerous when used as a weight-loss drug?
back: By uncoupling oxidative phosphorylation, DNP makes cells burn fuel rapidly and release the energy as heat instead of ATP, which does cause weight loss. But the uncontrolled heat production can cause fatal hyperthermia, and the collapse of ATP synthesis can itself be lethal. This narrow margin between fat burning and death is why DNP was banned for human use.

front: How does the mechanism of 2,4-dinitrophenol resemble the natural function of thermogenin in brown fat?
back: Both dissipate the proton gradient so that electron transport produces heat rather than ATP. DNP is a synthetic protonophore that ferries protons across the membrane, while thermogenin, uncoupling protein 1, is a regulated proton channel in the inner membrane of brown adipose tissue. Brown fat uses thermogenin deliberately for nonshivering heat production in infants and hibernating animals.

front: Why does oligomycin also slow electron transport, and how can an uncoupler restore oxygen consumption afterward?
back: Oligomycin blocks the F0 proton channel of ATP synthase, so protons cannot return to the matrix. The gradient builds until it opposes further pumping, and by respiratory control the chain slows and oxygen consumption falls even though the chain itself is not directly inhibited. Adding an uncoupler gives protons an alternate route, collapsing the gradient and letting electron transport and oxygen consumption resume, though still without making ATP.

front: What is the difference between state 3 and state 4 respiration, and what controls the switch?
back: State 3 is rapid respiration when ADP is plentiful: ATP synthase admits protons, so the chain runs fast and consumes oxygen quickly. State 4 is slow respiration when ADP is scarce and ATP is high: the gradient builds up and electron flow slows. This dependence of respiration rate on ADP availability is called respiratory or acceptor control and matches oxidation to energy demand.

front: Why does exporting ATP from the mitochondrion consume part of the proton-motive force?
back: The adenine nucleotide translocase exchanges matrix ATP carrying a 4-minus charge for cytosolic ADP carrying a 3-minus charge, a net outward movement of one negative charge that is driven by and partly discharges the membrane potential. The phosphate carrier then imports phosphate together with a proton, using the pH gradient. These transport steps are why roughly one extra proton per ATP is charged to the gradient, contributing to the figure of about 4 protons per ATP.

front: What is the central claim of the chemiosmotic theory that set it apart from the earlier chemical-coupling hypothesis?
back: The chemiosmotic theory, proposed by Peter Mitchell, holds that the energy linking electron transport to ATP synthesis is stored in a transmembrane proton gradient, not in a hypothetical high-energy chemical intermediate. The gradient is a delocalized energy currency, which explains why an intact sealed inner membrane is required and why proton-leaking uncouplers abolish ATP synthesis without blocking electron flow. No chemical intermediate coupling the two processes was ever isolated, which supported Mitchell.

front: How does the release of cytochrome c from mitochondria connect the electron transport chain to cell death?
back: Cytochrome c is a small water-soluble carrier that normally shuttles electrons from Complex III to Complex IV in the intermembrane space. When mitochondria are damaged and the outer membrane becomes permeable, cytochrome c escapes into the cytosol, where it helps assemble the apoptosome and activate caspases, triggering apoptosis. Thus a respiratory carrier doubles as a trigger of programmed cell death.

front: Why is FADH2 said to enter the electron transport chain at a lower energy level than NADH?
back: NADH donates electrons to Complex I, the highest-energy entry point, so its electrons traverse three proton-pumping complexes, I, III, and IV. FADH2, generated by Complex II and by the glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle, donates electrons to ubiquinone downstream of Complex I, so they pass only Complexes III and IV. Fewer pumping steps means fewer protons, about 6 versus 10, and therefore less ATP, about 1.5 versus 2.5.

front: Through what kind of receptor and mechanism does insulin trigger glucose uptake in muscle and fat?
back: Insulin binds a receptor tyrosine kinase that autophosphorylates and activates an intracellular cascade through IRS proteins and PI3-kinase. This causes vesicles carrying the GLUT4 transporter to fuse with the plasma membrane, sharply increasing glucose uptake in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue. Liver and brain use insulin-independent transporters such as GLUT2 and GLUT1 or GLUT3, so their glucose uptake does not depend on this step.

front: Why does glucagon act almost exclusively on the liver while epinephrine mobilizes fuel throughout the body?
back: Glucagon receptors are found mainly on hepatocytes, so glucagon chiefly directs the liver to perform glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis and to release glucose; skeletal muscle lacks glucagon receptors and does not respond. Epinephrine acts through widely distributed adrenergic receptors, so during stress it drives glycogenolysis and lipolysis in muscle, liver, and adipose at once. Both hormones raise cyclic AMP through Gs-coupled receptors, but their tissue reach differs.

front: How do insulin and glucagon produce opposite effects through cyclic AMP in the liver?
back: Glucagon and epinephrine activate adenylyl cyclase, raising cyclic AMP and activating protein kinase A, which phosphorylates enzymes to favor glycogen breakdown and gluconeogenesis. Insulin does the reverse: it activates phosphodiesterase to lower cyclic AMP and stimulates protein phosphatases, dephosphorylating the same enzymes to favor storage. The balance of phosphorylation, set by the insulin-to-glucagon ratio, decides whether the liver stores or releases fuel.

front: In the fed state, which key metabolic enzymes does insulin switch on by promoting dephosphorylation?
back: By favoring dephosphorylation, insulin activates glycogen synthase to store glucose as glycogen, pyruvate dehydrogenase to oxidize pyruvate, and acetyl-CoA carboxylase to make malonyl-CoA for fat synthesis. The same dephosphorylation inactivates glycogen phosphorylase and hormone-sensitive lipase, shutting down breakdown. The net result is a shift toward storing glycogen and fat.

front: In fasting, how does the glucagon-driven phosphorylation cascade redirect liver metabolism?
back: Glucagon raises cyclic AMP and protein kinase A activity, phosphorylating enzymes to activate glycogen phosphorylase and hormone-sensitive lipase while inactivating glycogen synthase and acetyl-CoA carboxylase. This turns on glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis, and fat mobilization and turns off storage. Falling malonyl-CoA also releases CPT-1, letting fatty acids enter mitochondria for oxidation and ketogenesis.

front: What is the ordered sequence of fuel sources the body uses as a fast progresses from hours to weeks?
back: For the first several hours after a meal, stored liver glycogen maintains blood glucose. Glycogen is largely depleted by roughly 24 hours, after which hepatic gluconeogenesis from lactate, glycerol, and amino acids becomes the main glucose source. Over days to weeks of starvation, adipose fatty acids and liver-made ketone bodies become the dominant fuel, sparing glucose and body protein.

front: Which tissues are obligate glucose users, and why?
back: Red blood cells are absolute glucose users because they lack mitochondria and depend entirely on glycolysis to lactate. The renal medulla and certain eye tissues such as the lens and cornea are also largely glucose-dependent because of poor oxygenation or lack of mitochondria. The brain normally consumes about 120 grams of glucose per day but, unlike the others, can adapt to burn ketone bodies during prolonged starvation.

front: Why does the shift to ketone bodies during prolonged starvation protect body protein?
back: Early in fasting the brain needs roughly 120 grams of glucose a day, much of it made by gluconeogenesis from muscle amino acids, which would rapidly consume protein. As starvation continues, the liver supplies ketone bodies that cover up to about two-thirds of the brain energy needs, sharply lowering its glucose requirement. Less gluconeogenesis means less amino acid breakdown, sparing muscle and vital protein and extending survival.

front: What is the glucose-fatty acid (Randle) cycle, and how does it spare glucose?
back: The Randle cycle describes how increased fatty acid oxidation in a tissue suppresses its use of glucose. Beta-oxidation raises acetyl-CoA, NADH, and citrate, which inhibit pyruvate dehydrogenase and phosphofructokinase-1 and let glucose-6-phosphate accumulate and slow hexokinase. By preferentially burning fat, tissues such as resting muscle spare glucose for glucose-dependent tissues during fasting.

front: How do respiratory quotient values reveal which fuel a tissue is oxidizing?
back: The respiratory quotient is CO2 produced divided by O2 consumed. Pure carbohydrate oxidation gives a value near 1.0 because it produces as much CO2 as the oxygen it uses, whereas fat oxidation gives about 0.7 because highly reduced fatty acids need extra oxygen relative to the CO2 they make. Protein is intermediate at about 0.8, and a typical mixed diet gives about 0.85.

front: How do the sizes of the body glycogen and fat energy reserves compare?
back: Triglyceride in adipose tissue is by far the largest reserve, on the order of 100,000 kcal in a typical adult, enough for weeks of fasting. Glycogen is a small but rapidly accessible reserve of only about 1,500 to 2,000 kcal, mostly in muscle, with the liver holding roughly one day of glucose. Mobilizable protein is a large but functionally precious store used mainly as a last resort.

front: Why must blood glucose be held within a narrow range, and what happens at the extremes?
back: Blood glucose is normally kept around 70 to 100 mg/dL because the brain and red blood cells rely on a steady glucose supply. Too little glucose rapidly impairs brain function, causing confusion, seizures, and coma, which is why glucagon, epinephrine, and cortisol all defend against hypoglycemia. Chronically high glucose, as in diabetes, damages tissues through glycation and osmotic effects, so insulin lowers it after meals.

front: Why can the brain not use fatty acids as fuel even though it can use ketone bodies?
back: Long-chain fatty acids travel bound to albumin and do not efficiently cross the blood-brain barrier, and the brain has little capacity for beta-oxidation. Ketone bodies, by contrast, are small water-soluble molecules that cross the barrier readily and are reconverted to acetyl-CoA inside neurons. This is why the liver makes ketone bodies during fasting: they are a transportable, brain-usable form of fatty acid energy.

front: By how much does the standard free energy change shift per tenfold change in a reaction's equilibrium constant at 25 C?
back: About 1.36 kcal/mol (5.7 kJ/mol) per factor of ten, because delta G naught prime equals -RT ln Keq and 2.303 times RT at 298 K is roughly 1.36 kcal/mol; each tenfold rise in Keq makes delta G naught prime about 1.36 kcal/mol more negative.

front: If a reaction's equilibrium constant is greater than 1, what is the sign of its standard free energy change?
back: Negative and thus exergonic, because delta G naught prime equals -RT ln Keq, so a Keq above 1 (products favored) gives a negative value, a Keq below 1 gives a positive value, and a Keq of exactly 1 gives zero.

front: What does the enthalpy term H contribute to a reaction's free energy?
back: Enthalpy is the heat content of the system, and delta H is the heat released or absorbed at constant pressure; a negative delta H (exothermic, favorable bond formation) promotes spontaneity while a positive delta H opposes it in delta G equals delta H minus T delta S.

front: How can a reaction that absorbs heat still be thermodynamically spontaneous?
back: An endothermic reaction with positive delta H can still have a negative delta G if it generates a large enough increase in entropy, since the minus T delta S term then outweighs the positive delta H, an effect that grows stronger at higher temperature.

front: Does adding an enzyme or catalyst change the free energy change or equilibrium constant of a reaction?
back: No; a catalyst only lowers the activation energy and speeds the approach to equilibrium in both directions equally, leaving delta G, delta G naught prime, and Keq unchanged because free energy is a state function set only by the initial and final states.

front: What must two reactions share for their free energy changes to be coupled?
back: A common intermediate, meaning the product of the exergonic reaction is a reactant of the endergonic one, so the steps can be summed into one pathway with a net negative delta G, as when a shared phosphorylated intermediate links ATP hydrolysis to an unfavorable step.

front: How does comparing the reaction quotient Q to Keq predict the direction a reaction proceeds?
back: From delta G equals delta G naught prime plus RT ln Q, when Q is less than Keq delta G is negative and the reaction runs forward, when Q is greater than Keq delta G is positive and it runs backward, and when Q equals Keq it is at equilibrium with delta G of zero.

front: Why would reaching true chemical equilibrium be fatal for a living cell?
back: At equilibrium delta G is zero, so no reaction can perform useful work; cells stay alive by remaining far from equilibrium as open systems, constantly importing nutrients and exporting products to keep key reactions exergonic.

front: What does a positive entropy change represent in a biochemical reaction?
back: A positive delta S reflects increased disorder or more available microstates, such as one molecule splitting into several or an ordered polymer being hydrolyzed to free monomers; the minus T delta S term then favors spontaneity.

front: What is the approximate standard free energy released when ATP's terminal phosphate is hydrolyzed to ADP and inorganic phosphate?
back: About -7.3 kcal/mol (-30.5 kJ/mol) under biochemical standard conditions; this moderately large negative value lets coupled reactions harness ATP to drive otherwise endergonic processes.

front: Why is the actual free energy of ATP hydrolysis in a living cell more negative than its standard value?
back: Cells maintain the ATP to ADP and phosphate ratio far above equilibrium, so the RT ln Q term drives the real delta G to roughly -11 to -13 kcal/mol, giving ATP greater driving power in vivo than the -7.3 kcal/mol standard figure suggests.

front: How many phosphoanhydride bonds does ATP contain compared with ADP and AMP?
back: ATP has two phosphoanhydride bonds (alpha to beta and beta to gamma), ADP has one, and AMP has none; AMP's single phosphate is attached to ribose by a lower-energy phosphoester bond.

front: Which phosphate group of ATP is transferred in a typical kinase reaction?
back: The terminal gamma phosphate; kinases move this outermost phosphoryl group onto an acceptor substrate, leaving ADP and a newly phosphorylated product.

front: Why is magnesium ion required for most reactions involving ATP?
back: The biologically active form is the Mg-ATP complex; Mg2+ coordinates the negatively charged phosphate oxygens, neutralizing charge repulsion and properly orienting the nucleotide in enzyme active sites.

front: How does ATP's bond to ribose differ energetically from the bonds between its phosphates?
back: The phosphate-to-ribose linkage is a phosphoester bond that yields little energy on hydrolysis, whereas the phosphate-to-phosphate linkages are high-energy phosphoanhydride bonds destabilized by charge repulsion and stabilized as products by resonance and solvation.

front: What enzyme hydrolyzes the pyrophosphate formed when ATP is split to AMP, and why does it matter?
back: Inorganic pyrophosphatase cleaves pyrophosphate into two inorganic phosphates; this additional strongly exergonic hydrolysis removes a product and pulls biosynthetic reactions such as DNA, RNA, and fatty acyl-CoA formation toward completion.

front: How does the store of phosphocreatine in resting muscle compare to ATP, and what does it provide?
back: Muscle holds several times more phosphocreatine than ATP, forming a rapid energy buffer that regenerates ATP through creatine kinase to power the first few seconds of intense exertion before glycolysis and oxidative phosphorylation catch up.

front: In what chemical form does NAD+ accept electrons, and what is released to solution?
back: NAD+ accepts a hydride ion (two electrons plus one proton) at C4 of its nicotinamide ring to become NADH, while the other hydrogen removed from the substrate is released as a free proton into the surrounding solution.

front: What single structural feature distinguishes NADP+ from NAD+?
back: NADP+ bears an extra phosphate group on the 2'-hydroxyl of its adenosine ribose; this small difference lets enzymes tell them apart, dedicating NADPH to biosynthesis and NADH to energy-yielding catabolism.

front: What is the approximate standard reduction potential of the NAD+/NADH couple, and what does it imply?
back: About -0.32 V; the negative potential marks NADH as a strong electron donor that readily passes electrons to carriers of more positive potential, which is why NADH enters the electron transport chain at its first complex.

front: Why is FAD considered a tightly bound prosthetic group while NAD+ acts as a mobile cosubstrate?
back: FAD and FMN stay firmly, sometimes covalently, attached to their flavoenzymes so their chemistry occurs on the enzyme, whereas NAD+ binds only transiently, is reduced to NADH, then diffuses away to deliver its electrons elsewhere.

front: How do the cell's NADH/NAD+ and NADPH/NADP+ ratios differ, and why?
back: The cell keeps NAD mostly oxidized (a low NADH/NAD+ ratio) so it can accept electrons in catabolic oxidations, but keeps NADP mostly reduced (a high NADPH/NADP+ ratio) so it can donate electrons for biosynthesis and antioxidant defense.

front: What deficiency disease results from inadequate dietary niacin, the precursor of NAD+ and NADP+?
back: Pellagra, classically marked by the three D's of dermatitis, diarrhea, and dementia; because niacin (vitamin B3) is needed to build NAD+ and NADP+, its lack impairs redox reactions across metabolism.

front: What defines a flavoprotein, and which vitamin supplies its cofactor?
back: A flavoprotein is an enzyme carrying a tightly bound flavin cofactor, FAD or FMN, made from riboflavin (vitamin B2); examples include succinate dehydrogenase and components of the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex.

front: What is meant by a reducing equivalent in metabolism?
back: A reducing equivalent is a transferable unit of reducing power, such as a single electron or a hydrogen atom (or a hydride carrying two electrons), shuttled by carriers like NADH and FADH2 from fuel oxidations to reductive reactions.

front: How do the overall shapes of catabolic and anabolic pathways differ?
back: Catabolism is convergent, funneling many different fuels into a few common intermediates such as acetyl-CoA, while anabolism is divergent, building many varied products from a small set of precursors, so a compact core supports wide biosynthetic variety.

front: Which redox carriers are characteristically used by catabolism versus anabolism?
back: Catabolism reduces NAD+ and FAD to NADH and FADH2 to capture energy for the electron transport chain, whereas anabolism relies on NADPH as its reducing agent, keeping degradative and biosynthetic electron flows separate.

front: Rank the three main modes of metabolic enzyme regulation by speed of response.
back: Allosteric effector binding is nearly instantaneous (milliseconds to seconds), covalent modification such as phosphorylation acts over seconds to minutes, and changing enzyme amount by altered synthesis or degradation is slowest, taking hours to days.

front: What specifically makes a reaction the committed step of a pathway?
back: The committed step is the first essentially irreversible reaction unique to that pathway, so once it occurs the substrate is dedicated to that pathway's product; this makes it the logical target for feedback regulation.

front: Why do opposing anabolic and catabolic pathways usually use different enzymes at their irreversible steps?
back: Separate enzymes at the irreversible steps let each direction be thermodynamically favorable and independently regulated, so the cell can activate one pathway while shutting off the other and avoid energy-wasting futile cycles.

front: What is metabolic flux?
back: Metabolic flux is the rate at which material flows through a pathway; it is governed mainly by the activity of the rate-limiting enzyme and is tuned to the cell's demand for the pathway's products or energy.

front: Why are far-from-equilibrium reactions, rather than near-equilibrium ones, used as regulatory control points?
back: Reactions with a large negative delta G run far from equilibrium and are effectively irreversible, so adjusting their enzyme's activity strongly changes pathway flux, whereas near-equilibrium reactions reverse easily and cannot impose such control.

front: How many ATP are produced gross in glycolysis before subtracting the amount invested?
back: Four ATP are made in the payoff phase (two by phosphoglycerate kinase and two by pyruvate kinase), but two are spent earlier by hexokinase and PFK-1, leaving a net yield of two ATP per glucose.

front: At which two glycolytic steps is ATP directly generated?
back: At the phosphoglycerate kinase step (1,3-bisphosphoglycerate to 3-phosphoglycerate) and the pyruvate kinase step (phosphoenolpyruvate to pyruvate); both are substrate-level phosphorylations occurring twice per glucose for four ATP total.

front: Which two glycolytic steps consume ATP, and what do they accomplish?
back: Hexokinase uses one ATP to form glucose-6-phosphate and PFK-1 uses another to form fructose-1,6-bisphosphate; this energy investment traps and destabilizes the sugar so the payoff phase can later extract more energy.

front: Which single reaction of glycolysis generates NADH?
back: The glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase reaction, which oxidizes glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate and reduces NAD+ to NADH while incorporating inorganic phosphate to make 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate; it runs twice per glucose for two NADH.

front: Which GLUT transporter is insulin-responsive, and where does it predominate?
back: GLUT4, concentrated in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue; insulin causes GLUT4-laden vesicles to fuse with the plasma membrane, sharply raising glucose uptake in the fed state.

front: How do the Km values of hexokinase and glucokinase differ, and why does it matter?
back: Hexokinase has a low Km near 0.1 mM so it stays saturated and active at low glucose in most tissues, while liver glucokinase has a high Km around 10 mM so it speeds up only when glucose is plentiful, letting the liver store excess after meals.

front: Why is PFK-1, not hexokinase, considered the committed step of glycolysis?
back: The glucose-6-phosphate made by hexokinase can still be diverted to glycogen synthesis or the pentose phosphate pathway, but the fructose-1,6-bisphosphate produced by PFK-1 is committed to proceeding through glycolysis, making PFK-1 the first committed step.

front: What is the product of the phosphofructokinase-1 reaction?
back: Fructose-1,6-bisphosphate; PFK-1 transfers a phosphate from ATP onto fructose-6-phosphate in an irreversible, rate-limiting reaction that commits the sugar to glycolysis.

front: Where does the phosphate on 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate come from?
back: From free inorganic phosphate in the cytosol rather than from ATP; GAPDH couples oxidation of glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate to attachment of this Pi, forming a high-energy acyl phosphate that later phosphorylates ADP.

front: Which glycolytic intermediate has the highest phosphoryl-transfer potential?
back: Phosphoenolpyruvate, whose phosphate hydrolysis is about -14.8 kcal/mol; after donating its phosphate the enol product tautomerizes to a stable keto form, making the pyruvate kinase reaction strongly favorable.

front: How does arsenate sabotage ATP formation in the payoff phase of glycolysis?
back: Arsenate mimics inorganic phosphate at GAPDH, producing an unstable arsenate ester that spontaneously breaks down; NAD+ is still regenerated but the phosphoglycerate kinase ATP is never made, uncoupling oxidation from phosphorylation.

front: Which glycolytic enzyme is inhibited by fluoride, making it useful in blood-glucose tubes?
back: Enolase; the fluoride in gray-top collection tubes inhibits enolase to stop glycolysis in drawn blood, preventing blood cells from consuming glucose and falsely lowering the measured value.

front: How can ATP be both a substrate and an inhibitor of PFK-1?
back: ATP binds the catalytic site as the phosphoryl donor, but when abundant it also occupies a separate allosteric site that lowers the enzyme's affinity for fructose-6-phosphate, so a high energy charge slows glycolysis.

front: Why does citrate inhibit PFK-1?
back: Elevated cytosolic citrate signals that the citric acid cycle is saturated and biosynthetic precursors are abundant, so citrate enhances ATP's allosteric inhibition of PFK-1 to slow further glucose breakdown.

front: How does AMP stimulate PFK-1?
back: AMP, which rises as ATP is consumed, binds PFK-1 and relieves the allosteric inhibition imposed by ATP, thereby accelerating glycolysis when the cell's energy charge falls.

front: Why does a drop in intracellular pH inhibit PFK-1?
back: Excess hydrogen ions, as accumulate during anaerobic lactate production, allosterically inhibit PFK-1 to slow glycolysis and protect the cell from dangerous lactic acidosis when oxygen is limited.

front: In the liver, how does glucagon lower fructose-2,6-bisphosphate?
back: Glucagon raises cAMP and activates PKA, which phosphorylates the bifunctional PFK-2/FBPase-2 enzyme; phosphorylation switches on FBPase-2 and off PFK-2, so fructose-2,6-bisphosphate falls, glycolysis slows, and gluconeogenesis rises.

front: How does insulin raise fructose-2,6-bisphosphate in the liver?
back: Insulin activates a phosphatase that dephosphorylates the bifunctional PFK-2/FBPase-2 enzyme, turning on PFK-2; the resulting rise in fructose-2,6-bisphosphate strongly activates PFK-1 and drives glycolysis in the fed state.

front: How does glucagon inhibit the liver isozyme of pyruvate kinase?
back: Glucagon-driven PKA phosphorylates the liver (L) form of pyruvate kinase, inactivating it so phosphoenolpyruvate is conserved for gluconeogenesis; the muscle (M) isozyme lacks this control site and stays active.

front: Why is pyruvate called a central metabolic branch point?
back: Pyruvate has several possible fates: aerobic decarboxylation to acetyl-CoA, anaerobic reduction to lactate, transamination to alanine, or carboxylation to oxaloacetate; the cell chooses among them based on oxygen, energy status, and biosynthetic needs.

front: Before lactate can be used for energy, what must first happen to it?
back: Lactate is a metabolic dead end and must be oxidized back to pyruvate by lactate dehydrogenase, which also regenerates NADH; the resulting pyruvate can then be oxidized in mitochondria or used by the liver for gluconeogenesis.

front: In alcoholic fermentation, which two enzymatic steps convert pyruvate to ethanol and regenerate NAD+?
back: Pyruvate decarboxylase removes CO2 to form acetaldehyde, then alcohol dehydrogenase reduces acetaldehyde to ethanol using NADH; the second step regenerates NAD+ so yeast glycolysis can continue without oxygen.

front: Why does hepatic fructose metabolism bypass the main control point of glycolysis?
back: Fructose is phosphorylated by fructokinase and cleaved by aldolase B into triose phosphates that enter glycolysis below PFK-1, so it escapes PFK-1 regulation; this unregulated entry can flood the pathway and promote lipogenesis and fatty liver.

front: What enzyme deficiency causes classic galactosemia, and what accumulates?
back: Deficiency of galactose-1-phosphate uridyltransferase (GALT) in the Leloir pathway; galactose-1-phosphate and galactitol build up, causing liver damage, cataracts, and intellectual disability unless dietary galactose is restricted.

front: How do dietary disaccharides such as lactose ultimately feed into glycolysis?
back: Brush-border enzymes hydrolyze disaccharides into monosaccharides (lactose into glucose plus galactose), which are absorbed and converted into glycolytic intermediates like glucose-6-phosphate before being broken down.

front: How many NADPH are produced per glucose-6-phosphate in the oxidative phase of the pentose phosphate pathway?
back: Two NADPH per glucose-6-phosphate: one from glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase and one from 6-phosphogluconate dehydrogenase, which also releases a CO2 to form ribulose-5-phosphate.

front: Which two dehydrogenases generate the NADPH of the PPP oxidative phase?
back: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase, the rate-limiting enzyme, and 6-phosphogluconate dehydrogenase; each reduces one NADP+ to NADPH, and the latter also decarboxylates its substrate to give ribulose-5-phosphate.

front: What reaction does glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase catalyze to start the pentose phosphate pathway?
back: It oxidizes glucose-6-phosphate to 6-phosphogluconolactone while reducing NADP+ to NADPH; this irreversible committed step sets flux through the whole pathway and is controlled by the NADP+/NADPH ratio.

front: Which carbon of glucose-6-phosphate is lost as CO2 in the pentose phosphate pathway?
back: The C1 carbon, released as CO2 during the 6-phosphogluconate dehydrogenase step, which converts the six-carbon substrate into five-carbon ribulose-5-phosphate.

front: How is ribulose-5-phosphate converted into the ribose-5-phosphate needed for nucleotides?
back: Ribose-5-phosphate isomerase rearranges ribulose-5-phosphate into ribose-5-phosphate; alternatively an epimerase converts it into xylulose-5-phosphate for the non-oxidative reactions.

front: What signals the cell to increase flux through the oxidative pentose phosphate pathway?
back: A high NADP+/NADPH ratio: as NADPH is consumed by biosynthesis or antioxidant defense, the rising NADP+ activates glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase, while abundant NADPH feedback-inhibits it.

front: Which glycolytic intermediates connect the non-oxidative pentose phosphate pathway to glycolysis?
back: Fructose-6-phosphate and glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate; transketolase and transaldolase interconvert pentose phosphates with these intermediates, letting carbon flow between the pathways as the cell requires.

front: What size carbon units do transketolase and transaldolase transfer?
back: Transketolase transfers two-carbon units and requires thiamine pyrophosphate, whereas transaldolase transfers three-carbon units; together they reshuffle sugars of three to seven carbons in the non-oxidative phase.

front: Which tissues have especially high pentose phosphate pathway activity, and why?
back: Tissues with heavy reductive biosynthesis or oxidative load: liver, adipose, lactating mammary gland, adrenal cortex, and gonads for fatty acid and steroid synthesis, plus red blood cells for antioxidant NADPH.

front: Besides fatty acid synthesis, what are key roles of the NADPH made by the pentose phosphate pathway?
back: NADPH regenerates reduced glutathione for antioxidant defense, fuels cytochrome P450 detoxification, drives cholesterol and steroid hormone synthesis, and powers the phagocyte NADPH oxidase respiratory burst.

front: How is reduced glutathione regenerated, and what does it protect against?
back: Glutathione reductase converts oxidized glutathione back to the reduced form using NADPH; reduced glutathione then lets glutathione peroxidase detoxify hydrogen peroxide and other reactive oxygen species, sparing cells from oxidative injury.

front: How do phagocytes use NADPH to kill ingested microbes?
back: The membrane enzyme NADPH oxidase passes electrons from NADPH to oxygen to make superoxide and downstream reactive oxygen species in the respiratory burst; loss of this enzyme causes chronic granulomatous disease with recurrent infections.

front: What triggers and blood-smear findings characterize a hemolytic episode in G6PD deficiency?
back: Oxidant stress from fava beans, antimalarials such as primaquine, sulfa drugs, or infection overwhelms the scarce NADPH; hemoglobin precipitates as Heinz bodies, and splenic pitting of these produces bite cells on the smear.

front: Why are red blood cells the cells most vulnerable in G6PD deficiency?
back: Red cells depend on the pentose phosphate pathway as their sole source of NADPH and, having no nucleus, cannot make new enzyme; when G6PD is deficient they cannot keep glutathione reduced and are lysed by oxidative stress.

front: Why might G6PD deficiency have persisted through human evolution?
back: It confers partial resistance to malaria, because Plasmodium parasites survive poorly in the oxidatively stressed, NADPH-poor red cells; this advantage in malaria-endemic regions offsets the risk of drug- or infection-triggered hemolysis.

front: In order, what four enzymes carry out the bypass reactions of gluconeogenesis?
back: Gluconeogenesis uses four enzymes to circumvent the three irreversible glycolytic steps: pyruvate carboxylase (pyruvate to oxaloacetate, in the mitochondrion), PEP carboxykinase (oxaloacetate to phosphoenolpyruvate), fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (fructose-1,6-bisphosphate to fructose-6-phosphate, the rate-limiting step), and glucose-6-phosphatase (glucose-6-phosphate to free glucose in the ER of liver and kidney). Pyruvate carboxylase and PEPCK together bypass the pyruvate kinase step.

front: Which three glycolytic steps are too exergonic to reverse, so gluconeogenesis must bypass them?
back: The three effectively irreversible, strongly exergonic glycolytic reactions are catalyzed by hexokinase or glucokinase (glucose to glucose-6-phosphate), phosphofructokinase-1 (fructose-6-phosphate to fructose-1,6-bisphosphate), and pyruvate kinase (phosphoenolpyruvate to pyruvate). The other seven near-equilibrium steps are simply run backward, so only these three large negative delta G steps need separate gluconeogenic enzymes.

front: How much ATP does pyruvate carboxylase use per reaction, and what one-carbon unit does it add?
back: Pyruvate carboxylase hydrolyzes one ATP to attach a carboxyl group derived from bicarbonate (HCO3-) onto pyruvate, forming four-carbon oxaloacetate. The CO2 is carried on a covalently bound biotin prosthetic group. This mitochondrial step both starts the conversion of pyruvate into glucose and replenishes citric acid cycle oxaloacetate (anaplerosis).

front: In gluconeogenesis, what happens to the carbon pyruvate carboxylase added once PEPCK acts?
back: PEP carboxykinase decarboxylates oxaloacetate, releasing the same CO2 that pyruvate carboxylase had just added, while transferring a phosphate from GTP to make phosphoenolpyruvate. This carboxylation-then-decarboxylation strategy is deliberate: adding CO2 builds the four-carbon oxaloacetate, and removing it supplies free energy that, together with GTP, creates the high-energy enol phosphate of PEP.

front: Which two enzymes convert glycerol into a gluconeogenic intermediate, and what is that intermediate?
back: Glycerol kinase phosphorylates glycerol to glycerol-3-phosphate using ATP, and glycerol-3-phosphate dehydrogenase oxidizes it to dihydroxyacetone phosphate (DHAP), producing NADH. DHAP is a triose phosphate shared by glycolysis and gluconeogenesis, so glycerol from triacylglycerol breakdown enters at the triose level. Glycerol kinase is present in liver but essentially absent from adipose tissue.

front: How is lactate turned into a gluconeogenic substrate, and what cofactor change accompanies it?
back: Lactate dehydrogenase oxidizes lactate back to pyruvate, reducing NAD+ to NADH. That cytosolic NADH is later needed to reverse the glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase step of gluconeogenesis. Lactate from muscle and red blood cells (delivered by the Cori cycle) is a major precursor precisely because it supplies both carbon and reducing equivalents.

front: Why does building glucose from pyruvate need cytosolic NADH, whereas using lactate does not?
back: Reversing glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase during gluconeogenesis consumes NADH, so two cytosolic NADH are used per glucose. When lactate is the precursor, its oxidation to pyruvate regenerates that NADH on the spot. When pyruvate or amino acids are the precursors, the reducing equivalents must instead be exported from the mitochondria as malate, so redox balance is a hidden constraint on which precursor is used.

front: How does a rising AMP level push fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase and PFK-1 in opposite directions?
back: AMP signals low energy charge: it allosterically inhibits fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (shutting down glucose synthesis) and activates phosphofructokinase-1 (turning on glycolysis). This reciprocal action keeps an ATP-poor cell from wasting energy making glucose. Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate imposes the same reciprocal pattern at this fructose-6-phosphate substrate cycle.

front: During fasting, how is liver pyruvate kinase controlled so gluconeogenesis is not immediately undone?
back: In fasting, glucagon raises cAMP and activates protein kinase A, which phosphorylates and inactivates liver pyruvate kinase. This stops newly made phosphoenolpyruvate from being converted straight back to pyruvate in a futile cycle. Pyruvate kinase is also inhibited by ATP and alanine and activated by fructose-1,6-bisphosphate, all of which favor gluconeogenesis when fuel is scarce.

front: Why must glucose-6-phosphate enter the endoplasmic reticulum for the final step of gluconeogenesis?
back: Glucose-6-phosphatase has its active site inside the lumen of the endoplasmic reticulum, so glucose-6-phosphate is carried in by a specific transporter (T1) before being hydrolyzed to free glucose plus inorganic phosphate. The products are then transported back out and glucose is released to the blood. Because only liver, kidney, and intestine express this ER system, only they can supply free glucose to the circulation.

front: How does cortisol support gluconeogenesis over a timescale of hours to days?
back: Cortisol is a glucocorticoid that increases transcription of gluconeogenic enzymes, especially PEP carboxykinase and glucose-6-phosphatase, raising their amounts. It also drives muscle proteolysis (supplying glucogenic amino acids) and lipolysis (supplying glycerol and fatty acids). This slow, transcription-based control complements the fast allosteric and glucagon-driven signals during prolonged stress or fasting.

front: Structurally, how does gluconeogenesis assemble one six-carbon glucose from three-carbon inputs?
back: Two three-carbon units (such as two pyruvate or two lactate) are converted upward to the triose phosphates dihydroxyacetone phosphate and glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate, which aldolase joins into six-carbon fructose-1,6-bisphosphate, and the pathway then proceeds to glucose. Gluconeogenesis therefore mirrors glycolysis as two three-carbon streams merging into one hexose, at a total cost of six high-energy phosphates and two NADH per glucose.

front: Why can propionyl-CoA from odd-chain fatty acids yield net glucose while acetyl-CoA cannot?
back: Propionyl-CoA (three carbons) is converted to succinyl-CoA, a citric acid cycle intermediate that can exit as malate and feed gluconeogenesis, giving a small net gain of glucose carbon. Acetyl-CoA (two carbons) is consumed in the cycle with both carbons lost as CO2 and cannot produce net oxaloacetate, so it yields no net glucose. Thus only the terminal three-carbon unit of an odd-chain fatty acid is glucogenic.

front: After an overnight fast, how does the source of blood glucose change as fasting continues?
back: Early in a fast the liver relies mainly on glycogenolysis, but hepatic glycogen is largely depleted within about 12 to 24 hours. As glycogen runs out, gluconeogenesis from lactate, glycerol, and amino acids becomes the dominant source of blood glucose. With prolonged starvation, ketone bodies increasingly replace glucose in peripheral tissues and the brain, lowering how much glucose must be synthesized.

front: What are the substrate (futile) cycles between glycolysis and gluconeogenesis, and why are they tolerated?
back: At the glucose/glucose-6-phosphate, fructose-6-phosphate/fructose-1,6-bisphosphate, and phosphoenolpyruvate/pyruvate steps, the opposing enzymes can run at the same time, hydrolyzing ATP with no net product. Cells minimize this waste through reciprocal allosteric and hormonal control so one direction predominates. A small amount of cycling is retained because it sharpens the sensitivity and speed of regulation and can generate heat.

front: About how often do alpha-1,6 branch points occur in glycogen, and why does this help?
back: A branch point occurs roughly every 8 to 12 glucose residues. This dense branching multiplies the number of nonreducing ends, where glycogen synthase and glycogen phosphorylase act, allowing very rapid storage and release of glucose. Branching also keeps the enormous molecule compact and water-soluble.

front: How is UDP-glucose made from glucose-1-phosphate, and what makes its synthesis irreversible?
back: UDP-glucose pyrophosphorylase joins glucose-1-phosphate with UTP to form UDP-glucose plus pyrophosphate. The reaction itself is freely reversible, but inorganic pyrophosphatase rapidly hydrolyzes the pyrophosphate to two phosphates, pulling the reaction forward. This coupling to pyrophosphate hydrolysis is what commits glucose to glycogen synthesis.

front: To which end of a glycogen chain does glycogen synthase add glucose, and what bond forms?
back: Glycogen synthase transfers the glucosyl group from UDP-glucose onto the nonreducing end of an existing chain, making a new alpha-1,4 glycosidic bond and releasing UDP. It can only extend a chain that is already several residues long, which is why the glycogenin protein primer is needed to start a molecule. It cannot form the alpha-1,6 branches, which require the separate branching enzyme.

front: What two separate catalytic activities does the glycogen debranching enzyme carry out?
back: The debranching enzyme is bifunctional. Its 4-alpha-glucanotransferase activity moves a block of three glucose residues from a branch onto a nearby nonreducing end, leaving a single glucose at the alpha-1,6 linkage. Its alpha-1,6-glucosidase activity then hydrolyzes that last residue, releasing free glucose and exposing the linear chain so glycogen phosphorylase can resume phosphorolysis.

front: During glycogenolysis, why is some glucose released free rather than as glucose-1-phosphate?
back: Glycogen phosphorylase produces glucose-1-phosphate by phosphorolysis of alpha-1,4 bonds, but the alpha-1,6-glucosidase activity of the debranching enzyme hydrolyzes each branch-point residue to free glucose. Because branch points occur about every 8 to 12 residues, roughly 8 to 10 percent of glycogen glucose emerges free and the remainder as glucose-1-phosphate. In muscle this free glucose is then phosphorylated by hexokinase for local use.

front: Why does glycogen phosphorylase stop about four residues short of each branch point?
back: Glycogen phosphorylase cannot cleave near an alpha-1,6 branch and halts four glucose residues away, leaving a highly branched core called a limit dextrin. The debranching enzyme must then remodel the branch before phosphorylase can continue. This is why complete glycogen degradation requires phosphorylase and debranching enzyme working together.

front: What distinguishes glycogen phosphorylase a from phosphorylase b?
back: Phosphorylase a is the phosphorylated, active form; phosphorylase b is the dephosphorylated, usually inactive form. Phosphorylase kinase converts b to a by phosphorylating a serine, and protein phosphatase-1 reverses it. In muscle, phosphorylase b can still be switched on allosterically by high AMP even without phosphorylation, while liver phosphorylase a is turned off allosterically when glucose binds it.

front: How does calcium released during muscle contraction directly turn on glycogen breakdown?
back: Phosphorylase kinase contains calmodulin as its delta subunit, so calcium released from the sarcoplasmic reticulum during contraction binds this calmodulin and partially activates phosphorylase kinase, even without any hormone. Activated phosphorylase kinase then converts glycogen phosphorylase b to a. This directly couples muscle contraction to on-demand glycogen mobilization for ATP.

front: How does glucose binding to liver glycogen phosphorylase promote a switch to glycogen synthesis?
back: When blood glucose is high, glucose binds liver phosphorylase a and shifts it to the tense, inactive conformation, exposing its phosphoserine to protein phosphatase-1. PP1 then dephosphorylates and inactivates phosphorylase and, at the same time, dephosphorylates and activates glycogen synthase. The liver thus senses adequate glucose and reciprocally shifts from breakdown to storage.

front: How can muscle mobilize glycogen during intense exercise with no hormonal signal?
back: In muscle, a sharp rise in AMP and inorganic phosphate from rapid ATP use allosterically activates phosphorylase b directly, and calcium from contraction activates phosphorylase kinase through its calmodulin subunit. Together these convert glycogen to glucose-1-phosphate at once, independent of epinephrine. This lets contracting muscle tap its glycogen the instant energy demand spikes.

front: Through which phosphatase does insulin simultaneously turn glycogen synthesis on and breakdown off?
back: Insulin signaling activates protein phosphatase-1 (PP1), which dephosphorylates the key enzymes. Dephosphorylation activates glycogen synthase while inactivating both glycogen phosphorylase and phosphorylase kinase. The coordinated result in the fed state is storage on and mobilization off.

front: How does glycogen synthase kinase-3 regulate glycogen synthesis, and how does insulin oppose it?
back: Glycogen synthase kinase-3 (GSK3) phosphorylates and inactivates glycogen synthase, restraining synthesis. Insulin, acting through the PI3K and Akt pathway, phosphorylates and inhibits GSK3, relieving that inhibition. With GSK3 off and PP1 on, glycogen synthase becomes dephosphorylated and active, promoting storage.

front: What is the cost, in high-energy phosphate bonds, of adding one glucose unit to glycogen?
back: It costs about two high-energy phosphate bonds per glucose stored. One ATP is spent phosphorylating glucose to glucose-6-phosphate by hexokinase or glucokinase, and one more high-energy bond is spent using UTP to form UDP-glucose, because the released pyrophosphate is hydrolyzed and the UDP must be re-phosphorylated by ATP. This modest cost buys storage in an instantly mobilizable form.

front: What unusual cofactor does glycogen phosphorylase use, and what does it do?
back: Glycogen phosphorylase uses pyridoxal phosphate (PLP), derived from vitamin B6. Unusually, it is the phosphate group of PLP, not the typical aldehyde, that participates, acting as a general acid or base to promote phosphorolysis of the glycosidic bond. This contrasts with the Schiff-base role PLP plays in amino acid metabolism.

front: Roughly how much glycogen does the liver store, and how long can it hold blood glucose in a fast?
back: The liver stores on the order of 100 grams of glycogen, about 5 to 6 percent of its wet weight when full. This reserve can maintain blood glucose for only about 12 to 24 hours of fasting before it is largely exhausted and gluconeogenesis must take over. Muscle stores more total glycogen, around 400 grams, but keeps it for its own use.

front: Which glycogen storage disease is caused by a defective debranching enzyme, and what accumulates?
back: Cori disease, also called Forbes disease or type III, results from a deficient glycogen debranching enzyme. Because only the outer alpha-1,4 chains can be removed, an abnormal glycogen with short outer branches, a limit dextrin, accumulates in liver and muscle. Fasting hypoglycemia and hepatomegaly occur but are usually milder than in von Gierke disease because outer chains still yield some glucose.

front: How many rounds of beta-oxidation does palmitate undergo, and how many acetyl-CoA form?
back: Palmitate is a 16-carbon saturated fatty acid, so it undergoes 7 rounds of beta-oxidation and yields 8 acetyl-CoA. Each round removes a two-carbon unit; the final round splits the last four-carbon unit into two acetyl-CoA, which is why there are 7 cycles but 8 acetyl-CoA.

front: How many FADH2 and NADH does palmitate beta-oxidation make before the acetyl-CoA enter the CAC?
back: The 7 rounds of palmitate beta-oxidation each make 1 FADH2 and 1 NADH, for 7 FADH2 and 7 NADH total. These are separate from the reduced cofactors produced later when the 8 acetyl-CoA are oxidized in the citric acid cycle. At about 1.5 ATP per FADH2 and 2.5 per NADH, this beta-oxidation stage alone yields roughly 28 ATP.

front: What is the net ATP yield from completely oxidizing one palmitate molecule?
back: Complete oxidation of palmitate yields about 106 net ATP (roughly 108 gross minus 2 for activation). Beta-oxidation gives 7 FADH2 (about 10.5 ATP) and 7 NADH (about 17.5 ATP); the 8 acetyl-CoA in the citric acid cycle give 24 NADH, 8 FADH2, and 8 GTP (about 80 ATP). Activation to palmitoyl-CoA costs 2 ATP equivalents. Older tables using 2 and 3 ATP per FADH2 and NADH give about 129 net.

front: Why does the last cycle of beta-oxidation yield two acetyl-CoA instead of one?
back: Beta-oxidation shortens the acyl chain by two carbons per cycle. When a four-carbon butyryl unit remains, the final thiolysis cleaves it into two acetyl-CoA at once. So an even-chain fatty acid of n carbons gives n/2 acetyl-CoA but only n/2 minus 1 cycles.

front: Why is fatty acid activation said to cost two ATP equivalents when only one ATP is used?
back: Acyl-CoA synthetase (thiokinase) cleaves ATP all the way to AMP plus pyrophosphate, not just to ADP plus phosphate. The pyrophosphate is then hydrolyzed to two phosphates, making activation irreversible. Because regenerating ATP from AMP takes two phosphorylations, the single reaction effectively spends two high-energy phosphate bonds.

front: Name in order the four enzymes that catalyze one cycle of beta-oxidation.
back: In order: acyl-CoA dehydrogenase oxidizes acyl-CoA to a trans-enoyl-CoA, making FADH2; enoyl-CoA hydratase adds water to form 3-hydroxyacyl-CoA; 3-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase oxidizes it to 3-ketoacyl-CoA, making NADH; and beta-ketothiolase cleaves it with CoA, releasing acetyl-CoA plus an acyl-CoA shortened by two carbons. The pattern is oxidation, hydration, oxidation, cleavage.

front: What are the roles of CPT-I, the translocase, and CPT-II in the carnitine shuttle?
back: Carnitine palmitoyltransferase I on the outer mitochondrial membrane swaps CoA for carnitine to form acylcarnitine; this is the rate-limiting, malonyl-CoA-inhibited step. Carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase moves acylcarnitine across the inner membrane in exchange for free carnitine. CPT-II on the matrix side swaps carnitine back for CoA, regenerating acyl-CoA inside the matrix for beta-oxidation.

front: Which fatty acids can enter the mitochondrion without the carnitine shuttle?
back: Short-chain and medium-chain fatty acids, roughly up to 10 to 12 carbons, diffuse freely across the mitochondrial membranes and are activated to acyl-CoA inside the matrix, bypassing the carnitine shuttle. Only long-chain fatty acids require CPT-I, the translocase, and CPT-II. This is why medium-chain triglycerides are useful for patients with carnitine shuttle defects.

front: What is the consequence of medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase (MCAD) deficiency?
back: MCAD deficiency blocks beta-oxidation of medium-chain fatty acids, so during fasting the body cannot fully oxidize fat or make enough ketone bodies. The classic result is hypoketotic hypoglycemia, because tissues keep burning glucose while fat energy is unavailable and the liver cannot produce ketones normally. It is one of the most common inherited fatty acid oxidation disorders and is included in newborn screening.

front: By what enzymes and cofactors is propionyl-CoA converted to succinyl-CoA?
back: Propionyl-CoA carboxylase, a biotin-dependent enzyme, adds CO2 to form D-methylmalonyl-CoA, which is epimerized to L-methylmalonyl-CoA, and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase, which needs vitamin B12 as adenosylcobalamin, rearranges it to succinyl-CoA. This pathway handles propionyl-CoA from odd-chain fatty acids and from valine, isoleucine, methionine, and threonine. B12 deficiency causes methylmalonic acid to build up.

front: In the fasting liver, what are the two fates of acetyl-CoA from beta-oxidation, and why do ketones form?
back: Acetyl-CoA from beta-oxidation can enter the citric acid cycle or be diverted to ketone body synthesis. During fasting, oxaloacetate is pulled away for gluconeogenesis, so the cycle cannot accept all the acetyl-CoA. The surplus acetyl-CoA is condensed into ketone bodies, capturing fat-derived energy in a form the liver can export to other tissues.

front: What is the rate-limiting enzyme of ketogenesis, and what intermediate does it make?
back: Mitochondrial HMG-CoA synthase is the committed, rate-limiting enzyme of ketogenesis; it condenses acetoacetyl-CoA with a third acetyl-CoA to form HMG-CoA (3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA). HMG-CoA lyase then cleaves HMG-CoA into acetoacetate plus acetyl-CoA. This mitochondrial pathway is distinct from the cytosolic HMG-CoA used for cholesterol synthesis.

front: What does a high beta-hydroxybutyrate to acetoacetate ratio reveal about the mitochondria?
